title: Accounts Merge
statement:
Given a list of `accounts` where each `accounts[i]` is a list of strings — the first element `accounts[i][0]` is a **name**, and the rest are **emails** of the account.

Merge the accounts. Two accounts belong to the same person if there is some **common email** to both. Even if two accounts have the same name, they may belong to different people, as people can have the same name. A person can have any number of accounts, but all of their accounts have the same name.

Return the accounts in the format: the first element of each account is the name, and the rest are emails **in sorted order**. The accounts themselves may be returned in any order.

**Example 1:**

```
Input: accounts = [["John","johnsmith@mail.com","john_newyork@mail.com"],["John","johnsmith@mail.com","john00@mail.com"],["Mary","mary@mail.com"],["John","johnnybravo@mail.com"]]
Output: [["John","john00@mail.com","john_newyork@mail.com","johnsmith@mail.com"],["Mary","mary@mail.com"],["John","johnnybravo@mail.com"]]
```

The first two Johns share `johnsmith@mail.com`, so they merge. The third John and Mary stay separate.

**Constraints:**

- `1 <= accounts.length <= 1000`
- `2 <= accounts[i].length <= 10`
- `1 <= accounts[i][j].length <= 30`
- `accounts[i][0]` consists of English letters.
- `accounts[i][j]` (for `j > 0`) is a valid email.

**Note on this simulation:** each account is a node. When two accounts share an email, they are **unioned** — a directed link is drawn from one root to the other, building a disjoint-set forest. For clarity the visual uses find WITHOUT path compression, so the forest you see is exactly the parent-pointer tree. Emails are grouped by their account's root, then sorted.
